title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a surf instructor called Liam talking about a beginner training programme. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a surf instructor called Liam talking about a beginner training programme. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: A surf instructor called Liam describes a beginner training programme.
extracts.audio_script: Hi. I’m Liam, one of the instructors at Bayview Surf Centre. We teach people to enjoy the sea safely, whether they hope to surf regularly or simply try a new sport during the summer. Our centre is beside a beach that used to be mainly known for fishing boats. The building we work from was originally a [9]lifeboat station[/9], which is appropriate because safety remains central to everything we do. It now contains changing rooms, an equipment store and a small classroom overlooking the beach.||Our beginner programme is aimed at teenagers and adults who have never surfed before. There is no need to buy a board or wetsuit. On the first morning, people are keen to get into the water, but we begin on land. The first lesson focuses on understanding [10]sea conditions[/10], including waves, tides and where it is safe to enter the water. We provide wetsuits and boards in different sizes. Participants should bring a towel and their own [11]swimming costume[/11] to wear underneath a wetsuit. Lockers are available for valuables.||Once instructors are happy that the sea is suitable, beginners practise in shallow water with close supervision. People assume that standing on the board is the first skill they will learn, but our initial practical exercise is [12]board control[/12], showing how to carry a board and stop it hitting other people in the water. Lessons normally take place at the southern end of the beach. If winds are strong there, we may move the session to the quieter [13]north bay[/13], where cliffs give some protection. Students are told about any change by text message before leaving home.||As well as lessons, the centre aims to care for the coast. All summer students can join our monthly [14]beach clean[/14], working alongside local residents to remove litter and record what is found. It is optional, but usually well attended. We also offer extra learning events. Last year the session on repairing surfboards was popular with experienced members. This summer, beginners have booked most places on our [15]wave photography[/15] workshop, which teaches them how to capture sea action safely from the shore, without taking cameras into the water.||Anyone completing five beginner sessions receives a [16]skills card[/16], showing which safety and surfing abilities have been achieved. This is useful when booking later sessions. At the end of August, students and instructors will take part in a friendly [17]surf competition[/17], with separate beginner and experienced groups; nobody is judged against professional standards. The centre also wants to improve access to the water. Money collected at our summer barbecue will help purchase a special [18]beach wheelchair[/18], allowing people with limited mobility to reach the firm sand more easily. Details and medical forms are available at reception.

dialogue.speaker: liam
dialogue.text: Hi. I’m Liam, one of the instructors at Bayview Surf Centre. We teach people to enjoy the sea safely, whether they hope to surf regularly or simply try a new sport during the summer. Our centre is beside a beach that used to be mainly known for fishing boats. The building we work from was originally a lifeboat station, which is appropriate because safety remains central to everything we do. It now contains changing rooms, an equipment store and a small classroom overlooking the beach.||Our beginner programme is aimed at teenagers and adults who have never surfed before. There is no need to buy a board or wetsuit. On the first morning, people are keen to get into the water, but we begin on land. The first lesson focuses on understanding sea conditions, including waves, tides and where it is safe to enter the water. We provide wetsuits and boards in different sizes. Participants should bring a towel and their own swimming costume to wear underneath a wetsuit. Lockers are available for valuables.||Once instructors are happy that the sea is suitable, beginners practise in shallow water with close supervision. People assume that standing on the board is the first skill they will learn, but our initial practical exercise is board control, showing how to carry a board and stop it hitting other people in the water. Lessons normally take place at the southern end of the beach. If winds are strong there, we may move the session to the quieter north bay, where cliffs give some protection. Students are told about any change by text message before leaving home.||As well as lessons, the centre aims to care for the coast. All summer students can join our monthly beach clean, working alongside local residents to remove litter and record what is found. It is optional, but usually well attended. We also offer extra learning events. Last year the session on repairing surfboards was popular with experienced members. This summer, beginners have booked most places on our wave photography workshop, which teaches them how to capture sea action safely from the shore, without taking cameras into the water.||Anyone completing five beginner sessions receives a skills card, showing which safety and surfing abilities have been achieved. This is useful when booking later sessions. At the end of August, students and instructors will take part in a friendly surf competition, with separate beginner and experienced groups; nobody is judged against professional standards. The centre also wants to improve access to the water. Money collected at our summer barbecue will help purchase a special beach wheelchair, allowing people with limited mobility to reach the firm sand more easily. Details and medical forms are available at reception.
